evidence: Adapter-specific tool protocols are repeatedly redeclared as thin composites with little behavioral differentiation (e.g., `LangGraphToolsProtocol`, `PydanticAIToolsProtocol`, `ParlantToolsProtocol`, `AnthropicToolsProtocol`) in `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py`, and `src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`.

Adapter construction/config wiring is duplicated across adapters via near-identical `*AdapterConfig` + `from_config` pass-through patterns (`src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py`), and tests mirror this duplication (`tests/adapters/test_pydantic_ai_adapter.py`, `tests/adapters/test_crewai_adapter.py`).

Contact strategy flow uses callback-mediated round-trips between `PlatformRuntime` and `ContactEventHandler` (`src/thenvoi/runtime/platform_runtime.py` and `src/thenvoi/runtime/contacts/contact_handler.py`), creating a multi-hop wrapper chain for hub-event injection and broadcasts.

`src/thenvoi/runtime/types.py` enforces only `on_event is not None` for CALLBACK mode, while `src/thenvoi/runtime/contacts/contact_handler.py` always `await`s it; current tests in `tests/runtime/contacts/test_contact_config.py` cover presence/absence but not non-async callback misuse.

`src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/handler.py` models `HandlerResult.status` as a `Literal` only; downstream handling in `src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/route_dispatch.py` treats unknown statuses as success, and `tests/bridge/test_router.py` covers only handled/ignored/error paths.

`src/thenvoi/runtime/types.py` includes `is_agent_sender_type`, used in execution/formatting/conversion paths, but `tests/runtime/test_types.py` currently covers `normalize_handle` and `format_for_llm` only, leaving this high-fanout helper without direct regression tests.

Runner startup/orchestration flow is repeated with near-identical structure across docker and example entrypoints (env parsing, prompt composition, adapter construction, agent lifecycle) in docker/claude_sdk/runner.py, docker/codex/runner.py, and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py.

Framework adapters repeat the same structural scaffolding (adapter-specific ToolsProtocol declarations and from_config pass-through constructors) across src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py, src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py, src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py, and src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py.

Tool integration logic is split into multiple framework-specific wrapper builders while a shared dispatcher already exists, creating parallel representations of the same platform tool contract in src/thenvoi/runtime/tool_bridge.py, src/thenvoi/integrations/claude_sdk/tools.py, and src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py.

The tool surface is concentrated in a broad object/protocol pair (src/thenvoi/runtime/tools.py + src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py) and mirrored in test fake implementation (src/thenvoi/testing/fake_tools.py), coupling unrelated responsibilities (messaging, participant management, contacts, memory, schema export, dispatch).

findings.summary: Per-adapter tool protocol aliases create parallel interfaces with minimal semantic differences, increasing maintenance without adding real extension value.
evidence: `LangGraphToolsProtocol`, `PydanticAIToolsProtocol`, and `ParlantToolsProtocol` each mostly restate `MessagingToolsProtocol + ToolDispatchProtocol` rather than defining materially distinct contracts.
`AnthropicToolsProtocol` adds one schema method, but still follows the same per-adapter pattern, producing N parallel protocol types for one underlying tool surface.
This fragmentation propagates into adapter generics and test doubles instead of a single stable capability model.
findings.suggestion: Replace adapter-local protocol aliases with a small shared set of capability protocols (e.g., `DispatchTools`, `MessagingDispatchTools`, `AnthropicSchemaTools`) in `core/protocols.py`, and have adapters type against those shared contracts directly.

findings.summary: Adapter config objects and `from_config` constructors are repeated as mostly mechanical pass-through wrappers, causing change amplification across frameworks.
evidence: Each adapter defines a dataclass config and a `from_config` classmethod that forwards nearly every field one-to-one into `__init__`.
Behavioral policy is rarely added in `from_config`; most code is field mapping boilerplate, so API evolution requires synchronized edits in multiple adapters and tests.
Test coverage includes repeated `from_config` checks per adapter, reflecting structural duplication rather than distinct domain behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared config-loading mechanism (e.g., `@classmethod from_mapping` on a common adapter base or a reusable mapper utility) and keep adapter-specific config only for truly unique fields/validation.

findings.summary: Contact handling relies on callback indirection between runtime and handler, creating a leaky boundary and wrapper chain with temporal coupling.
evidence: `PlatformRuntime._setup_contact_handling()` injects callbacks (`_queue_broadcast`, `_inject_hub_event`, `_inject_hub_system_prompt`) into `ContactEventHandler`.
`ContactEventHandler` then routes through sink callbacks to call back into runtime-owned execution contexts for hub-room operations, instead of targeting a dedicated runtime-facing interface.
The behavior depends on ordering (`initialize_hub_room`, runtime execution creation, `mark_hub_room_ready`), indicating a boundary that is orchestrationally coupled rather than explicitly modeled.
findings.suggestion: Define a dedicated runtime contact port/interface (e.g., `ContactRuntimePort`) implemented by `PlatformRuntime`, and make `ContactEventHandler` depend on that explicit port instead of multiple callbacks plus sink indirection.

findings.summary: CALLBACK contract is only partially tested, missing non-async callback failure coverage
evidence: `ContactEventConfig.__post_init__` checks only that `on_event` is present for CALLBACK strategy, not that it is awaitable.
`ContactEventHandler._handle_callback` unconditionally does `await self._config.on_event(...)`, so a sync callback fails at runtime.
Current tests validate missing callback raises and async callback works, but do not include sync/non-awaitable callback cases.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit async-contract validation (for example, reject non-coroutine callbacks in `ContactEventConfig`) and add tests for sync callback rejection plus runtime behavior when invalid callbacks slip through.

findings.summary: Handler result API allows invalid statuses and tests miss malformed-status behavior
evidence: `HandlerResult.status` is type-hinted as `Literal[...]`, but dataclass construction does not enforce this at runtime.
`execute_dispatch_targets` handles only `ignored` and `error`; any other status falls through as success.
Router tests verify `handled`, `ignored`, and `error` outcomes but do not test malformed statuses from custom handlers.
findings.suggestion: Enforce runtime status validity (Enum or `__post_init__` guard) and add negative tests proving malformed statuses are rejected or mapped to explicit failures instead of implicit success.

findings.summary: High-impact sender-type normalization helper lacks direct unit tests
evidence: `is_agent_sender_type` in `runtime/types.py` controls case-insensitive agent detection logic.
This helper is consumed in message-to-role conversion paths (`runtime/formatters.py`) and execution flow, but no direct test references `is_agent_sender_type`.
`tests/runtime/test_types.py` currently exercises `normalize_handle` and `PlatformMessage.format_for_llm`, not sender-type normalization edge cases.
findings.suggestion: Add parameterized tests for sender type normalization (`None`, whitespace, mixed case, unknown types) and one behavior-level assertion in formatting/execution tests to lock downstream role-mapping behavior.

findings.summary: Quality-gate governance is documented but not enforced as a single executable contract.
evidence: README.md states CI-required contract checks (bridge routing, bootstrap contracts, prompt contracts, integration marker policy).
pyproject.toml defines pytest defaults/markers, but there is no contract declaration that asserts README governance claims remain aligned with test configuration.
tests/support/integration/config.py repeats integration execution semantics in docstrings and policy code, creating multiple drift points for governance behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one governance contract test module that parses/validates the README quality-gate section against canonical constants from test configuration (marker policy, integration command shape, required contract suites), and source README snippets from those constants where possible.

findings.summary: Integration-mode detection relies on substring matching of `-m` expression text.
evidence: In `_is_integration_mode`, integration detection is based on text checks (`"notintegration"` and `"integration"` substring membership after whitespace removal) rather than marker-token semantics.
pyproject.toml default `addopts = "-v -m 'not integration'"` makes this parser path central to policy behavior; expression variants can be misclassified by substring heuristics.
findings.suggestion: Replace substring logic with marker-aware evaluation (parse mark expression tokens or derive integration mode from collected test items/markers), and add targeted tests for mixed expressions to prevent false positives/negatives.

findings.summary: Test event factories use inconsistent default timestamp baselines across event types.
evidence: `make_message_event`/room helpers default to `2024-01-01...` while contact-related helpers default to `2026-01-01...`.
README.md positions contract checks as PR quality gates, but this shared fixture module introduces avoidable chronology skew that can mask ordering assumptions in cross-event tests.
findings.suggestion: Standardize all default fixture timestamps to one canonical baseline constant (or require explicit timestamps in ordering-sensitive tests) and add a small contract test asserting factory defaults remain coherent.

findings.summary: Runner entrypoints duplicate orchestration flow, causing convention and behavior drift risk
evidence: All three runner files perform the same high-level sequence (read config/env, compose prompt, create adapter, create agent, run lifecycle), but each maintains its own env contract and defaults.
docker/claude_sdk/runner.py and examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py are especially close structurally yet diverge in required mounts and role/workspace handling.
Shared helper functions exist in runner_core, but orchestration decisions remain hand-assembled per file, so changes to startup policy require synchronized edits across entrypoints.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a declarative `RunnerSpec` + single generic runner executor in `src/thenvoi/testing/runner_core.py` (adapter factory, env schema, mount policy, optional repo init hooks), and keep each entrypoint as thin configuration-only wiring.

findings.summary: Adapter modules repeat the same protocol/config scaffolding instead of a shared adapter pattern
evidence: Multiple adapters define near-identical `*ToolsProtocol` classes that primarily restate messaging + tool dispatch requirements.
Multiple adapters include a `from_config` classmethod that is largely parameter forwarding to `__init__`, repeating boilerplate mapping logic per adapter.
The repeated structural template suggests one intended adapter lifecycle pattern, but the pattern is implemented manually in each module.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared adapter construction scaffold (e.g., typed config protocol + reusable config-to-init utility and common tool-contract aliases) so each adapter only defines framework-specific behavior rather than repeating setup shape.

findings.summary: Framework-specific tool wrappers duplicate behavior already centralized in tool_bridge
evidence: tool_bridge already normalizes args, dispatches tool calls, and builds consistent failure contracts.

claude_sdk/tools.py and parlant/tools.py still build separate per-tool wrapper functions (`_build_send_message_tool`, `_build_send_event_tool`, `_build_add_participant_tool`, etc.) with framework-local parsing/result shaping and error branches.
When platform tool semantics change, updates are needed in centralized bridge logic and multiple wrapper layers, increasing drift probability.
findings.suggestion: Define a single declarative tool descriptor table (name, arg schema adapter, response formatter) and generate framework-specific wrappers from that table while keeping dispatch and error semantics exclusively in `tool_bridge`.

findings.summary: AgentTools centralizes too many unrelated responsibilities behind one broad protocol
evidence: `AgentToolsProtocol` combines messaging, participants, contacts, memory, schema export, and generic dispatch into one interface.
`AgentTools` implements all of these concerns in one class with many public async methods and internal helper responsibilities.
`FakeAgentTools` mirrors the same broad surface, forcing tests to carry large fake behavior even when only a narrow capability subset is required.
findings.suggestion: Split tool capabilities into composable service objects (messaging/participants/contacts/memory/schema-dispatch) and provide a thin aggregator only where required; migrate adapters to depend on the narrowest protocol slice they actually use.
